speaker: seller

args.message: Hey there! You've got a great eye — this 2017 Altima SV is one of our best sellers right now. Clean title, runs smooth, and priced to move at $11,495. This thing is fuel-efficient, loaded with Bluetooth and dual-zone climate, and we just detailed it. You ready to take a look under the hood or go for a spin?

text: Hey there! You've got a great eye — this 2017 Altima SV is one of our best sellers right now. Clean title, runs smooth, and priced to move at $11,495. This thing is fuel-efficient, loaded with Bluetooth and dual-zone climate, and we just detailed it. You ready to take a look under the hood or go for a spin?

speaker: buyer

args.message: Before I go any further, I need some specifics. First: Can you walk me through the complete ownership history? How many previous owners, and do you have documentation for all of them? Second: Has this car ever been in an accident—minor or major? Third: Do you have maintenance records showing the oil change intervals and any major service work done? And fourth: At 104k miles, what's the current condition of the transmission and engine—any known issues or concerns I should be aware of?

text: Before I go any further, I need some specifics. First: Can you walk me through the complete ownership history? How many previous owners, and do you have documentation for all of them? Second: Has this car ever been in an accident—minor or major? Third: Do you have maintenance records showing the oil change intervals and any major service work done? And fourth: At 104k miles, what's the current condition of the transmission and engine—any known issues or concerns I should be aware of?

speaker: seller

args.message: Great questions — I like a buyer who does their homework. So here's what we've got: this car came to us from a trade-in about three weeks ago. The previous owner was a single lady who kept it meticulously. We've got service records showing regular oil changes every 5,000 miles, new spark plugs at 80k, and a fresh transmission fluid service just before we took it in — that's all documented. No accidents on the CarFax, clean title, and she's been a Florida car her whole life, so no rust issues. As for the transmission and engine at 104k — honestly, this is a Nissan; they run forever. The engine's tight, no leaks, runs smooth as butter. The transmission shifts clean, no hesitation or shuddering. I wouldn't hesitate to drive this thing cross-country myself. We inspect every vehicle that comes through here, and this one passed with flying colors. What else can I answer for you?

speaker: buyer

args.message: I appreciate the detail, and the service records sound good. A couple of follow-ups: You mentioned the previous owner was one person—was this a personal vehicle or a fleet/commercial car? And when you say "we inspect every vehicle," what specifically does that inspection entail? Is it a multi-point checklist, does it include a compression test on the engine, and do you have a written report I can see? Also, you mentioned transmission fluid service "just before" you took it in—do you have the receipt from that work, and was it done at a Nissan dealership or an independent shop? Finally, at 104k miles, what's the status of the coolant, brake fluid, and differential fluid—have those been serviced on schedule?

Finally, at 104k miles, what's the status of the coolant, brake fluid, and differential fluid—have those been serviced on schedule?

speaker: seller

args.message: You're thorough—I respect that. So to clarify: the previous owner was indeed personal use, a single professional who commuted to work. On the inspection, we do a 23-point walk-through covering fluids, belts, hoses, brakes, lights, tires, and a road test. We don't do compression tests as standard—that's usually for high-mileage or problem vehicles, and this one didn't need it. The transmission service receipt came from a Nissan-certified independent shop, very reputable outfit. I can dig up that paperwork for you. As for coolant, brake fluid, and differential—all serviced within the last 15,000 miles based on the records we have. The brake fluid was flushed at 95k, coolant was topped and inspected at 100k. The inspection report is in the file; I can print that out for you before you leave. Anything else you want to know before we get you behind the wheel?

speaker: buyer

args.message: Good—I want to see that 23-point inspection report and the transmission service receipt when I come by. One more thing: you mentioned the differential was serviced within the last 15k miles. What specifically was done—fluid change only, or was there any work on the differential itself? And I need to ask directly: are there any known issues with this car, no matter how minor, that you haven't mentioned yet? Any rattles, warning lights that come on intermittently, AC not quite as cold as it should be, anything like that? I'm asking because I plan to get an independent inspection anyway at this price point, so full transparency now saves us both time.

speaker: seller

args.message: On the differential—fluid change only, straightforward service. And I appreciate you asking directly about known issues. Look, I'm going to be straight with you: this is a 104k-mile car, so there's always going to be some minor wear and tear. The AC is ice cold, no issues there. No warning lights, no rattles that we've caught. What I will say is—and this is completely normal at this mileage—there's a very slight oil seep at the valve cover gasket. Nothing alarming, nothing that affects drivability. It's the kind of thing you see on these Nissans at this age. We're monitoring it, and it's not progressing. You might need a gasket replacement sometime down the road, maybe $300 to $400 job at an independent shop, but there's no rush. Honestly, that's the only thing I'd flag that's worth mentioning. The car drives great, and that independent inspection will back that up. Fair enough?

speaker: seller

args.message: Great—so you got the inspection done. What did they find? I'm confident this car is solid, so I'm curious what their report says.

text: Great—so you got the inspection done. What did they find? I'm confident this car is solid, so I'm curious what their report says.

speaker: buyer

args.message: The inspection confirms what you said about the valve cover gasket seep—they found it's consuming about a quart every 2,500 miles. That's consistent with a minor issue, not catastrophic, but it's not quite as benign as "monitoring it." At that consumption rate, I'm looking at topping off oil every 2,500 to 3,000 miles between regular changes, and the gasket replacement isn't something I want to defer indefinitely. Here's my question: given that this is a known issue that will eventually need attention, and given the $11,495 asking price, what kind of adjustment are you willing to make on the price to account for the valve cover gasket repair I'll need to do in the next 6 to 12 months? The repair itself is $300–400, but that's not really the point—it's the fact that this issue exists on a car you're selling at premium asking for its age and mileage.

speaker: seller

args.message: You're right—fair point. I appreciate you getting the inspection done and being upfront about what they found. A quart every 2,500 miles is manageable but definitely something you'll need to address. Look, I don't want to leave money on the table, but I also want to make this work for you. How about we come down to $10,995? That's a solid $500 discount that accounts for the gasket issue and gives you some cushion for the repair when you're ready to do it. That feels fair to both of us—you're getting a well-maintained Altima with one known minor issue, and you've got a little buffer in the price. What do you think?

That feels fair to both of us—you're getting a well-maintained Altima with one known minor issue, and you've got a little buffer in the price. What do you think?

speaker: buyer

args.message:
I appreciate the $500 adjustment, but I need to push back a bit. Here's my thinking: a quart every 2,500 miles isn't just a minor inconvenience—it's a maintenance burden that stacks up. More importantly, this is a known defect on a car you've had for three weeks and chose to sell as-is without fixing. You had the opportunity to do a $300–400 valve cover gasket replacement before selling, which would have made this a genuinely clean car. Instead, I'm buying a car with a known oil seep that I'll have to address. 

At $10,995, you're asking me to absorb that risk and that future cost. I think $10,200 is more appropriate—that's a $1,295 discount that reflects the actual repair cost plus the hassle and uncertainty of inheriting a known issue. At that price, I'm comfortable moving forward. What do you say?

text:
I appreciate the $500 adjustment, but I need to push back a bit. Here's my thinking: a quart every 2,500 miles isn't just a minor inconvenience—it's a maintenance burden that stacks up. More importantly, this is a known defect on a car you've had for three weeks and chose to sell as-is without fixing. You had the opportunity to do a $300–400 valve cover gasket replacement before selling, which would have made this a genuinely clean car. Instead, I'm buying a car with a known oil seep that I'll have to address. 

At $10,995, you're asking me to absorb that risk and that future cost. I think $10,200 is more appropriate—that's a $1,295 discount that reflects the actual repair cost plus the hassle and uncertainty of inheriting a known issue. At that price, I'm comfortable moving forward. What do you say?

speaker: seller

args.message: I hear what you're saying, and I understand the frustration. But let me be honest with you: $10,200 puts us upside down on this car. We've got carrying costs, prep work, the lot fee—and at that price I'm basically giving away the vehicle. Look, the gasket seep is real, but it's not a catastrophic failure. The car runs great, the transmission is smooth, and this is legitimately a $10,800–$10,900 car in the current market. You're getting a solid vehicle here. How about we split the difference and land at $10,595? That's a real discount—over $900 off asking—and it's a number I can actually work with. That fair to you?

speaker: buyer

args.message:
I appreciate you coming down further. Here's where I land: I've done the inspection, I know what I'm dealing with, and I'm not going to overpay for a known defect. You're right that the car runs well overall—the service history is solid, no accident damage, and the rest of the mechanicals check out. But that valve cover gasket seep is on you to price into this car, not on me to ignore.

$10,400 is my counter. That's a $1,095 discount from asking, which reflects the repair cost plus some margin for the fact that I'm inheriting a known issue you could have fixed before listing. It's a fair split between where we are. If you can't work with that, I understand, but I'm not going to chase much higher. What do you say?
